title: Stratified licensing-measurement candidate
notes: Second-round candidate generated after critiques of the licensing-ideology and opportunity-preemption candidates. It is a typed assembly for testing construct hygiene across evidence, licensing, production, ideology, and measurement channels. It is not a preferred theory and carries zero scores pending critique.

edges.rationale: Observed frequency depends on how many contexts could have hosted the form.
Opportunity mass controls how strongly absence, rarity, or presence bears on licensing.
Usage frequency is evidence for licensing only after opportunity structure is considered.
Analogical support can be evidence that a form belongs near a licensed constructional neighborhood without equaling licensing.
For operator-rich cases only, a missing or mis-set operator value may be a component of licensing failure; this is a scoped theoretical wager, not a definition of licensing in general.
Analogical pressure can make innovative or marginal forms producible before their licensing is settled.
Forms licensed by a community are more available for ordinary production in relevant contexts.
Licensing state predicts pressure toward repair, reformulation, or substitution.
Acceptability reports can serve as noisy measurements of licensing when task, ideology, processing, and context effects are modeled separately.
Recoverable intended meanings can raise acceptability reports without licensing the form.
Processing difficulty can depress acceptability reports independently of licensing.
Instructional exposure can strengthen or activate standard-language ideology.
Standard-language ideology can generate explicit rule-based condemnation.
Explicit condemnation predicts institutional or pedagogical correction.
Genre conditions which forms are appropriate in the activity type.
Register or genre mismatch can lower acceptability reports without changing licensing.
Speaker cues can shift judgment responses for socially indexed or dialectally marked forms.
Indexical associations can affect acceptability reports while remaining distinct from operator value and licensing.
Correctness ideology can depress acceptability reports for licensed but stigmatized or condemned forms.
Task framing can cue correctness, naturalness, register fit, or community-membership criteria.
Community licensing is evidence for grammaticality attribution, but not identical to the attribution.
Acceptability reports inform attribution only as noisy evidence.
Correction probability is evidence about attributed status, but remains separable from licensing because correction can be ideological.
Repair pressure is evidence about perceived licensing trouble without being the same as grammaticality attribution.
